messages.say:
Thought for 1m 39s


Verified in 2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTIONARY.json:


header_count_expected is 144.

headers contains 144 key/value pairs (i.e., 144 “properties”).

header_order contains 144 items.
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…


01999000042260124527_COMPLETE_S…


2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…


What “headers as an object with 144 properties” means


In JSON:


An object is { ... } and contains named fields (also called properties or keys).

So "headers": { ... } is a single object.

Inside it, each header name is a property name (key), like "absolute_path" or "allocated_at".

Each property’s value is that header’s definition object (with value_schema, scope, presence, normalization, provenance, etc.).
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…


Example from the file (conceptually):


headers["absolute_path"] → returns the definition for the absolute_path column/header.
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…


This is not an array like ["absolute_path", "allocated_at", ...] where you’d use numeric indexes. With an object, you access by name (key).


Why there’s also header_order


JSON objects are conceptually unordered, so the file also provides header_order as the authoritative column sequence (and it lists all 144 headers).


2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

Thought for 1m 24s


According to a document from 2026-01-28, each header entry under headers must define the same 5 sections (same shape for all 144 headers): value_schema, scope, presence, normalization, provenance.


2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…


For each header name (example: absolute_path), you define:


value_schema
The allowed data type/shape for the column’s value (a JSON-Schema-like fragment). In this file it can include things like:
type (often ["string","null"], or "string", or ["object","null"], etc.)

optional constraints like format, pattern, enum, minimum, maximum, items
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

scope
Where this header is valid/expected to appear:
record_kinds_in: which record kinds this column applies to

entity_kinds_in: which entity kinds it applies to (when relevant)
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

presence
Whether the value is required and under what conditions:
policy: one of REQUIRED, OPTIONAL, CONDITIONAL

rules: an array (empty when not conditional) of rule objects that can include:
rule_id, severity, when (condition), must (requirement)
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

normalization
How to normalize/transform the value:
transforms: an array of transform names (can be empty)
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

provenance
Where this header definition came from:
sources: array of { doc_id, path, anchor } entries (can be empty)
2026012816000001_COLUMN_DICTION…

Thought for 6m 31s


Thought for 38s


Verified.


headers contains 144 properties (one per header name): len(headers)=144

header_count_expected=144

len(header_order)=144


What “headers as an object with 144 properties” means


In your JSON, headers is a JSON object (map/dictionary), not a list/array.


Each property name under headers is the header name (e.g., "absolute_path", "file_id").

Each property’s value is that header’s definition object.


So you look up definitions by name:


headers["absolute_path"] (or dot notation in some languages)


What must be defined for each header


Every header entry under headers.<header_name> defines the same 6 blocks:


value_schema — allowed type(s) + constraints (type, pattern, enum, etc.)

scope — where it applies (record_kinds_in, entity_kinds_in)

presence — REQUIRED/OPTIONAL/CONDITIONAL + conditional rules

normalization — transforms to apply to raw values

provenance — which source docs/anchors justify the definition

derivation — how the value is populated (input/extracted/lookup/derived/system), including the actual process spec (task output key, lookup path template, or formula AST) plus evidence expectations


“We need a file that defines each field… don’t rewrite file”


Created a companion glossary file that defines every structural field used by the column dictionary (top-level fields + per-header definition fields + derivation/process/rules fields):


Download 2026012816000002_COLUMN_DICTIONARY_FIELD_GLOSSARY.json


Note: 11 headers currently contain to_be_specified placeholders inside derivation (they are the ones using DERIVED + FORMULA_AST), meaning their exact source column(s)/formula still need to be finalized.

Thought for 5s


You need two record types worth of headers to capture that pipeline cleanly:


Per-file analysis (single script) — outputs from Stage 0–2 (normalize, AST extract, semantic facts).

Per-comparison analysis (A vs B) — outputs from Stage 1–3 (diffs, scores, tests, evidence).


Below are the column headers to create, grouped by what data you said you need to capture. Names are in snake_case to match your dictionary style.



A) Headers for per-file analysis records


Identity + provenance


analysis_run_id — unique ID for this analysis execution

analyzed_at_utc — timestamp

toolchain_id — stable identifier for tool set (ast-only, libcst, etc.)

tool_versions — JSON object: {python, formatter, analyzer, pytest, coverage, ruff, mypy, ...}

source_repo_id — repo identifier (or registry reference)

source_commit_sha — commit being analyzed

source_branch — optional

source_file_id — your registry file_id

source_relative_path — path at time of analysis

source_absolute_path — optional (prefer relative)

source_file_hash_raw — hash of raw bytes

source_file_size_bytes


Stage 0 — normalization outputs


text_encoding_detected

newline_style_original — LF|CRLF|MIXED

newline_style_normalized — usually LF

bom_present — boolean

utf8_normalized — boolean

canonical_text_hash — hash after normalization

formatter_used — NONE|BLACK|OTHER

formatter_config_hash — hash of formatter config (or null)

canonical_format_hash — hash after formatting (if any)

normalization_warnings — JSON array of issues


Stage 1 — AST extraction outputs


ast_parse_ok — boolean

ast_parse_error — string/null

ast_dump_hash — whole-file normalized AST dump hash

ast_node_count_total

ast_node_counts_by_type — JSON object {FunctionDef: n, Call: n, Try: n, ...}

ast_imports_list — JSON array of import strings or structured entries

ast_imports_hash

defs_functions_count

defs_classes_count

defs_top_level_assignments_count


Per-definition fingerprints (function/class granularity)


defs_index_artifact_path — path to JSON mapping qualname → def metadata

defs_hashes_artifact_path — path to JSON qualname → signature_hash/body_hash

defs_public_api_list — exported/public defs (JSON array)

defs_public_api_hash


Stage 2 — semantic facts (cheap signals)


call_graph_artifact_path — JSON (caller → callees)

external_calls_artifact_path — JSON list of external libs/apis used

constants_artifact_path — JSON list of constants/config literals

exceptions_raised_list — JSON list

exceptions_caught_list — JSON list

try_except_count

io_surface_flags — JSON object booleans: {file_read, file_write, network, subprocess, db, env_read}

io_surface_details_artifact_path — JSON facts (paths, env var names, endpoints if detectable)

global_state_mutation_flags — JSON booleans (module globals mutated, singleton writes, etc.)

semantic_facts_hash — hash of the extracted facts bundle


Evidence for per-file analysis


analysis_report_json_path

analysis_evidence_bundle_path — logs/jsonl

analysis_success — boolean

analysis_errors — JSON array



B) Headers for per-comparison records (File A vs File B)


Identity + inputs


comparison_run_id

compared_at_utc

comparison_scope — single_file|multi_file|repo_subset

left_file_id / right_file_id

left_commit_sha / right_commit_sha

left_canonical_text_hash / right_canonical_text_hash

left_ast_dump_hash / right_ast_dump_hash

comparator_version


Stage 1 — structural diff results (AST-based)


structural_diff_ok — boolean

structural_similarity_score — 0–1

ast_diff_summary — short JSON (counts of add/remove/modify)

functions_added_count

functions_removed_count

functions_modified_count

classes_added_count

classes_removed_count

classes_modified_count

signatures_changed_count

control_flow_changed_count — if/for/while/try changes detected

api_call_nodes_changed_count

structural_changes_artifact_path — detailed JSON


Stage 2 — semantic diff results (facts deltas)


semantic_similarity_score — 0–1

public_api_added — JSON array

public_api_removed — JSON array

dependency_imports_added — JSON array

dependency_imports_removed — JSON array

external_calls_added — JSON array

external_calls_removed — JSON array

exceptions_raised_added / exceptions_raised_removed

exceptions_caught_added / exceptions_caught_removed

io_surface_change_flags — JSON object (what changed)

global_state_mutation_change_flags

semantic_changes_artifact_path — detailed JSON


Stage 3 — behavioral comparison (tests/coverage/traces)


tests_executed — boolean

pytest_exit_code

tests_passed_count

tests_failed_count

tests_skipped_count

test_report_artifact_path

coverage_enabled — boolean

coverage_percent_left

coverage_percent_right

coverage_diff_percent

coverage_report_artifact_path

runtime_trace_enabled — boolean

runtime_trace_artifact_path

golden_files_used — boolean

golden_diff_artifact_path


Scoring + gating


text_similarity_score — 0–1

overall_similarity_score — 0–1

behavioral_risk_score — 0–100 (or 0–1)

risk_level — low|medium|high|critical

risk_reasons — JSON array of triggered heuristics

recommended_actions — JSON array (run tests, require review, block merge, etc.)

gate_decision — PASS|WARN|FAIL

gate_rule_ids_triggered — JSON array


Evidence for comparison


comparison_report_json_path

comparison_evidence_bundle_path

comparison_errors — JSON array



C) Optional headers if you want repo-wide (multi-file) comparisons


These are needed when your comparison is across a whole folder/repo rather than a single file:


file_set_left_count

file_set_right_count

files_added_count

files_removed_count

files_modified_count

aggregate_structural_similarity

aggregate_semantic_similarity

aggregate_risk_score

top_risk_files_artifact_path — ranked list

dependency_graph_diff_artifact_path



D) One header you explicitly asked for earlier: derivation/process


If you want the column dictionary itself to encode “how this column is populated,” add a per-header dictionary field (not a column in the registry):


derivation — structured “mode + sources + process spec + evidence” (formula AST / lookup spec / task-output binding)


That’s how you avoid “free text” and keep it enforceable.



If you want this to align with your existing registry patterns, implement two new record_kind values in scope.record_kinds_in (or equivalent): PY_FILE_ANALYSIS and PY_FILE_COMPARISON, so the same column dictionary can validate both datasets without mixing meanings.

Why you need these headers at all


They turn the “analyze Python scripts deterministically” pipeline into queryable, enforceable evidence. Without headers, you have unstructured JSON blobs that you can’t reliably:


diff across runs,

gate merges,

audit why a file was flagged,

reproduce results,

or aggregate risk across a repo.


Headers are the index. Artifacts (JSON reports) are the payload.



Value delivered by each header group



A. Identity + provenance headers


Headers: analysis_run_id, analyzed_at_utc, toolchain_id, tool_versions, source_repo_id, source_commit_sha, source_branch, source_file_id, source_relative_path, source_file_hash_raw, source_file_size_bytes


Value:


Reproducibility: you can rerun and prove you used the same toolchain/version.

Traceability: tie every result to the exact file, path, repo, commit.

Non-ambiguous evidence: prevents “this report is about which file/version?” problems.

Auditability: supports “why did this change get flagged” weeks later.


If you skip these: you’ll get reports you cannot trust or reconcile across time.



B. Stage 0 (Normalization) headers


Headers: text_encoding_detected, newline_style_original, newline_style_normalized, bom_present, utf8_normalized, canonical_text_hash, formatter_used, formatter_config_hash, canonical_format_hash, normalization_warnings


Value:


Noise reduction: eliminates false differences caused by encoding/newlines/formatting.

Deterministic comparison: canonical hashes mean “same content after normalization” is provable.

Explains weird diffs: normalization warnings tell you when the pipeline had to “fix” input.


If you skip these: text diffs and AST diffs become harder to interpret and reproduce.



C. Stage 1 (AST extraction) headers


Headers: ast_parse_ok, ast_parse_error, ast_dump_hash, ast_node_count_total, ast_node_counts_by_type, ast_imports_list, ast_imports_hash, defs_functions_count, defs_classes_count, defs_top_level_assignments_count


Value:


Refactor-resistant signal: AST ignores whitespace/comment noise.

Fast change detection: ast_dump_hash tells you “structure changed” instantly.

Explainability: node counts and imports show what kind of change happened.

Hard failures visible: parse_ok/error is essential; otherwise failures get hidden.


If you skip these: you lose the main “meaningful diff” layer and can’t classify changes reliably.



D. Per-definition fingerprints (function/class granularity)


Headers: defs_index_artifact_path, defs_hashes_artifact_path, defs_public_api_list, defs_public_api_hash


Value:


Diff at the unit that matters: functions/classes, not files.

Stable matching: you can match by qualname and detect add/remove/modify without “moved code” noise.

API stability detection: public_api_hash is a quick gate for breaking changes.


If you skip these: everything devolves to coarse “file changed” with poor diagnostics.



E. Stage 2 semantic facts (behavior-likely-change signals)


Headers: call_graph_artifact_path, external_calls_artifact_path, constants_artifact_path, exceptions_raised_list, exceptions_caught_list, try_except_count, io_surface_flags, io_surface_details_artifact_path, global_state_mutation_flags, semantic_facts_hash


Value:


Meaningful risk detection: these identify changes that are likely to alter behavior or security posture.

Targeted review: “network/subprocess/env usage changed” is a high-value alert.

Cheap static security hints: external calls + IO surface + env vars are key.


If you skip these: you can detect changes but can’t prioritize which changes matter.



F. Evidence headers


Headers: analysis_report_json_path, analysis_evidence_bundle_path, analysis_success, analysis_errors


Value:


Deterministic gating: a gate needs “where is the proof artifact” and “did the run succeed.”

Debbugability: errors stored as data, not lost in logs.


If you skip these: you can’t enforce “NO_IMPLIED_BEHAVIOR” because there’s no explicit evidence pointer.



Why comparison headers exist (A vs B)



Structural diff headers (Stage 1)


Headers: structural_similarity_score, counts of added/removed/modified defs, signatures_changed_count, control_flow_changed_count, structural_changes_artifact_path


Value:


Classifies change type: add/remove vs modify; signature vs control-flow.

Scoring: objective similarity metric for automation.

Evidence pointer: to the detailed diff.



Semantic diff headers (Stage 2)


Headers: API/import/external call/exceptions/IO deltas + artifact path


Value:


“Did behavior likely change?” answered directly and consistently.

Risk prioritization: you can flag only meaningful semantic deltas.



Behavioral headers (Stage 3 tests/coverage/traces)


Headers: tests_executed, pytest_exit_code, test counts, coverage metrics, trace/golden file artifact paths


Value:


Confidence layer: AST+facts suggest risk; tests/coverage confirm or refute.

Release gating: “run tests if risk high” becomes enforceable.



Scoring + gating headers


Headers: text_similarity_score, overall_similarity_score, behavioral_risk_score, risk_level, risk_reasons, gate_decision, gate_rule_ids_triggered


Value:


Automated decisions: merge gates require structured outcomes.

Explainable gates: risk_reasons + rule_ids is why it passed/failed.



What value they deliver together (the real reason)



Together, these headers create a closed loop:


Input identity is precise (file_id/commit/hash)

Noise is neutralized (normalization)

Structure is measured (AST)

Meaning is inferred (semantic facts)

Behavior is validated (tests/coverage optional)

Decision is enforceable (scores + gate_decision)

Evidence is retrievable (artifact paths + run_id)


That combination gives you:


Deterministic gating (no subjective reviews required to block obvious risk)

Fast triage (rank changes by risk, not by file size)

Repeatable audits (same input → same outputs; any deviation explained by tool_versions/config hashes)

Portfolio-level reporting (aggregate risk across a repo without rereading JSON blobs)

Regression prevention (detect subtle breakage patterns: API signature changes, IO surface expansion, new subprocess calls)



If you want the “minimum viable” header set



If you don’t want the full set, the minimum that still delivers high value is:


Per-file:


analysis_run_id, analyzed_at_utc, tool_versions

source_file_id, source_commit_sha, source_file_hash_raw

canonical_text_hash, ast_dump_hash

defs_public_api_hash

io_surface_flags

analysis_report_json_path, analysis_success


Per-comparison:


comparison_run_id, compared_at_utc

left_file_id, right_file_id, left_commit_sha, right_commit_sha

structural_similarity_score, signatures_changed_count, control_flow_changed_count

io_surface_change_flags

behavioral_risk_score, gate_decision, risk_reasons

comparison_report_json_path


Everything else is “increase diagnostic depth / reduce manual investigation.”



If you want this aligned to your column-dictionary philosophy, the value proposition is simple: headers = enforceable index; artifacts = detailed proof. Without the index, you can’t gate, aggregate, or audit.

Yes. The header sets I listed enable exactly that consolidation workflow, but only if you bias them toward “deliverables/capabilities” rather than generic code metrics.


Below is the mapping from your objective → the headers that make it possible, plus the minimum “deliverables + overlap” header set you actually need.



What you’re trying to answer


What does this script deliver? (outputs, side effects, interfaces, responsibilities)

Which scripts deliver the same thing? (overlap/duplicates)

Which one is best? (select canonical version)

What breaks if we consolidate? (dependencies + API surface)


To do that, you need deliverable fingerprints, capability facts, and quality ranking signals.



Why these headers enable overlap + “best version” selection


1) Deliverables fingerprinting (detect “same deliverable”)


You need a stable, refactor-resistant signature for “what it does,” not “what it looks like.”


Core headers:


deliverable_signature_hash
Deterministic hash of extracted “deliverables facts” (see below). Enables grouping duplicates.

deliverable_kinds (JSON array)
e.g. ["cli_tool","library_module","daemon","migration_script","validator"]

deliverable_outputs (JSON array)
Files produced, registries updated, reports emitted, DB tables touched.

deliverable_side_effects (JSON object / flags)
{writes_registry:true, writes_disk:true, network:true, subprocess:false, ...}

deliverable_interfaces (JSON array)
CLI entrypoints, public functions/classes, exported API (__all__), config schema keys.

deliverable_inputs (JSON array)
argv flags, env vars read, config files read, input file patterns, stdin usage.


Value: These make “same deliverable” computable and clusterable.



2) Overlap analysis (prove two scripts overlap and how)


Core headers:


capability_tags (JSON array)
Controlled vocabulary like: ["registry_update","id_assignment","path_resolve","schema_validate","watcher","orchestrator"]

capability_facts_hash
Hash of structured extracted facts (imports/calls/IO surface/exceptions/config keys).

overlap_group_id (computed)
Group key = deliverable_signature_hash or similarity cluster ID.

overlap_similarity_score (0–1)
Based on structural + semantic similarity (AST + facts).


Value: You can sort by “same job” and quantify degree of overlap.



3) “Best version” selection (ranking within an overlap group)


You need objective ranking signals; otherwise “best” becomes subjective.


Core headers:


quality_score (0–100)
Composite, deterministic (tests + lint + complexity + docs + risk).

test_status / tests_executed / pytest_exit_code

coverage_percent

static_issues_count (ruff/mypy/flake8 counts)

security_risk_flags (network/subprocess/env/secrets patterns)

maintainability_metrics (complexity, function length, duplication)

last_modified_at (from git, if available)

owner_subsystem / intended_subsystem (declared classification)


Value: Within each overlap cluster, you can pick the canonical candidate and justify it.



4) Consolidation safety (what breaks if we delete/merge)


Core headers:


imports_in_repo (who imports this)

exports_public_api (what it exposes)

call_graph_artifact_path (detailed, not in-column)

dependency_graph_artifact_path

config_keys_read / env_vars_read

paths_written / registries_written


Value: You can compute blast radius and update references deterministically.



The minimum “deliverables + overlap + best version” header set


If you want a tight set that directly supports your goal, create these headers (per Python file):


Identity + provenance (required)


file_id

relative_path

git_commit_sha

file_hash_raw

analyzed_at_utc

tool_versions


Deliverables (required)


deliverable_kinds

deliverable_signature_hash

deliverable_outputs

deliverable_inputs

deliverable_interfaces

deliverable_side_effects


Capability facts (required)


capability_tags

capability_facts_hash

io_surface_flags

external_calls_list

imports_list


Overlap + consolidation (required)


overlap_group_id

overlap_similarity_score

imports_in_repo_count

imported_by_list_artifact_path (store list in artifact; header points to it)


Best-version ranking (recommended)


quality_score

tests_executed

pytest_exit_code

coverage_percent

static_issues_count

security_risk_flags


Evidence pointers (required)


analysis_report_json_path

analysis_success

analysis_errors


That set is enough to:


cluster duplicates,

see what each script delivers,

and select a canonical version with defensible criteria.



What “deliverable_signature_hash” should be based on (so it works)


To detect “same deliverable,” build the signature from stable facts, not formatting:


Include (normalized):


declared deliverable kind

outputs written (paths/patterns/registry targets)

inputs (argv/env/config keys)

IO surface flags

exported interfaces (CLI commands, public API symbols)

key external integrations (requests/boto3/sqlite/subprocess)


Exclude:


line order, comments, formatting

local variable names

docstrings


Result: refactors won’t change the deliverable signature unless behavior/output contract changes.



Bottom line


Your statement is correct: these headers are valuable specifically because they make “deliverables,” “overlap,” and “best version” computable. Without them, you can’t reliably cluster duplicates or justify consolidation decisions.


If you want, I can produce a strict controlled vocabulary for deliverable_kinds and capability_tags so clustering doesn’t degrade into inconsistent free-text tagging.
